turns.en: Today this is going to be an ask me anything episode. I'm joined with my friends Trenton Bricken and Sholto Douglas. You guys do some AI stuff, right? Um, double um, they're researchers at Anthropic. Other news: I have a book launching today. It's called The Scaling Era. Yeah, um, I hope one of the questions ends up being why you should buy this book, but we can kill two birds with one stone. But um, okay, let's just get at it. What's the first question that we got to answer?
turns.zh: 今天这期是问答节目。我和我的朋友 Trenton Bricken 和 Sholto Douglas 一起。你们搞 AI 的对吧？嗯，他们是 Anthropic 的研究员。另外，我今天有一本书要发布，叫《Scaling 时代》。嗯，我希望其中一个问题是为什么你应该买这本书，但我们可以一石二鸟。不过嗯，好吧，我们直接开始。第一个要回答的问题是什么？

turns.en: Take us away. So I want to ask the flyball question that I heard before: why should ordinary people care about this book? Like, why should my mom buy and read the book?
turns.zh: 开始吧。我想问一个我之前听过的快问快答问题：为什么普通人应该关心这本书？比如，我妈妈为什么要买来读？

turns.en: Yeah, first let me tell you about the book, what it is. So you know these last few years I've been interviewing AI lab CEOs, researchers, people like you guys obviously, but also scholars from all kinds of different fields, economists, philosophers. And they've been addressing, I think, what are basically the gnarliest, most interesting, most important questions we've ever had to ask ourselves. Like, what is the fundamental nature of intelligence? What will happen when we have billions of extra workers? How do we model out the economics of that? Um, how do we think about an intelligence that is greater than the rest of humanity combined? Is that even a coherent concept? And so what I'm super delighted with is that with Stripe Press we made this book where we compiled and curated the best, most insightful snippets across all these interviews. And you can read Dario addressing wider scaling work, and then on the next page is Demis explaining DeepMind's plans for whether they're going to go with the RL route and how much the AlphaZero stuff will play into the next generation of LLMs. And on the next pages, of course, you guys going through the technical details of how these models work. Um, and then there's so many different fields that are implicated. I mean, I feel like AI is one of the most multi-disciplinary fields that one can imagine, because there's no field, no domain of human knowledge that is not relevant to understanding what a future society of different kinds of beings will look like. Um, there's, you know, you have like Carl Sagan talk about how the scaling hypothesis shows up in primate brain scaling from chimpanzees to humans. On the next page might be an economist trying to argue, like Tyler Cowen explaining why he doesn't expect explosive economic growth and why the bottlenecks will eat all that up. Um, anyways, so that's why your mom should buy this book. It just, it is the distillation of all these different fields of human knowledge applied to the most important questions that humanity is facing right now.
turns.zh: 首先让我介绍一下这本书是什么。过去几年我一直在采访 AI 实验室的 CEO、研究人员，显然包括你们，还有来自各个领域的学者、经济学家、哲学家。他们讨论的，我认为是我们必须问自己的最棘手、最有趣、最重要的问题。比如，智能的本质是什么？当我们有数十亿额外劳动力时会发生什么？如何建模其经济学？如何思考比人类总和更聪明的智能？这个概念本身是否连贯？我很高兴的是，和 Stripe Press 一起，我们制作了这本书，从所有这些采访中精选了最好、最有洞见的片段。你可以读到 Dario 讨论更广泛的 Scaling 工作，下一页是 Demis 解释 DeepMind 的计划，是否走强化学习路线，以及 AlphaZero 在下一代大语言模型中的作用。再下一页当然是你们讲解这些模型的技术细节。还有涉及如此多的领域。我觉得 AI 是你能想象的最跨学科的领域之一，因为没有哪个领域、哪个人类知识领域与理解未来不同智能体构成的社会无关。比如，卡尔·萨根讨论 Scaling 假说如何体现在从黑猩猩到人类的灵长类大脑 Scaling 中。下一页可能是一位经济学家，比如泰勒·考恩解释为什么他不预期爆炸性经济增长，以及瓶颈会如何吞噬这一切。总之，这就是为什么你妈妈应该买这本书。它正是所有这些人类知识领域应用于人类当前面临的最重要问题的精华。

turns.en: I do like how the book is sliced up by different topics and across interviews. Yeah, yeah, so it does seem like a nice way to listen to all of the interviews in one digestible way. There's um two interviews I've done that haven't been released publicly before that are in the book. So one was that Jared Kaplan, um uh who's one of your co-founders, and this is another example where it's like he's a physicist and he's explaining scaling from this like very mathematical perspective about data manifolds. Um uh, and then on the same next page you have like a totally different perspective, it's like Goren talking about you know why why why can't we just have like distilled, like why did general intelligence actually evolve in the first place, what is the actual evolutionary purpose of it. Um, and it's like page by page right, you can just get addresses. Even for me, I mean like the person who's been on the other end of these conversations, it was actually really cool to um like read it and just be like oh I actually like I now I realize how these insights connect to each other.
turns.zh: 我确实喜欢这本书按不同主题和跨采访切片的方式。是的，这似乎是一种很好的方式，可以以易于消化的形式收听所有采访。书中有两段我之前未公开发布的采访。一个是 Jared Kaplan，他是你们的联合创始人之一，另一个例子是，他作为物理学家，从数据流形等非常数学的角度解释 Scaling。然后同一页下一页是完全不同的视角，比如 Goren 谈论为什么我们不能只是蒸馏，为什么通用智能最初会进化，它的实际进化目的是什么。就这样一页一页地，你可以直接得到这些见解。即使对我来说，作为这些对话的另一端，读起来也真的很酷，就像哦，我现在意识到这些见解是如何相互关联的。

turns.en: Yeah, the only other thing that stood out to me as well is the introduction section. The only thing that stood out to you? Yeah, yeah, that was that was really the only thing that was noteworthy. Um, I just mean in terms of accessibility, yeah, is the introduction section and the diagrams for like all the different inputs that enable you to train a machine learning model. Stripe Press books are also just side captions for explaining what parameters are, what a model is, these sorts of things. Actually, when we did our episode together, a bunch of people, I don't know if you saw this, independently made these like blog posts and Anki cards where they were explaining things because we just kind of passed over some things. Um, and hopefully we've given a similar treatment to every single interview I've done, where you can read a very technical interview with a lab CEO or something or an engineer or a researcher, and then this side is like here, here, here's more context, here's more definitions, here's more commentary. Um, and yeah, I feel like it elevated the conversations. So in other words, my parents will finally understand why I do fridge all they do they it very well. Maybe my parents are all I need to know is that my name's in a book. You're a co-author. They're like cool. Should we get into the questions?
turns.zh: 是的，另外唯一让我印象深刻的是引言部分。唯一让你印象深刻的部分？是的，那真的是唯一值得注意的部分。嗯，我是指从可访问性角度，引言部分和那些关于训练机器学习模型所需各种输入的图表。Stripe Press 的书还有侧边注释，解释什么是参数、什么是模型之类的东西。实际上，当我们一起做那期节目时，很多人，我不知道你是否看到，独立制作了博客文章和 Anki 卡片来解释一些我们一带而过的东西。嗯，希望我对每一期采访都做了类似处理，你可以读一篇非常技术性的采访，比如与实验室 CEO 或工程师或研究员的对话，然后旁边有更多背景、更多定义、更多评论。嗯，我觉得这提升了对话的质量。换句话说，我的父母终于能理解为什么我整天搞 AI 了。也许我父母只需要知道我的名字出现在书里。你是合著者。他们会觉得酷。我们开始回答问题吧？

ts.sec: Question on Models Not Making Cross-Domain Connections

turns.en: All right, so Brian Krav asks: the issue you raise with Dario and occasionally tweet about relating to models not making connections across disparate topics, some sort of combinatorial attention challenge. What are your thoughts on that now? Do you solve it with scale, thinking models, or something else?
turns.zh: 好的，Brian Krav 问：你和 Dario 提到过，偶尔也在推特上讨论过的问题，关于模型无法在不同主题之间建立联系，某种组合注意力挑战。你现在怎么看？是通过 Scaling、思维模型还是其他方式解决？

turns.en: By the way, so the issue is one of the questions asked Dario: look, these models have all of human knowledge memorized, and you would think if a human had this much stuff memorized um and they were moderately intelligent, they could be making all these connections between different fields. And there are examples of humans doing this by the way. Um, there's Donald Swan or something like this. This guy noticed that the way what happens to your brain after magnesium deficiency is exactly uh the kinds of I don't know structure you see during a migraine. So he's like okay, you take magnesium supplements and we're going to cure a bunch of migraines, and it worked. And there are many other examples of things like this where you just like notice two different connections between pieces of knowledge. Why, if these LLMs are intelligent, are they not able to use this unique advantage they have to make these kinds of discoveries? Um, I feel a little shy like me giving answers on AI with you guys here. But so actually Scott Alexander addressed this question in one of his AMA threads, and he's like look, humans also don't have this kind of logical omniscience, right? He gave the example of in language: if you really thought about like why are two words connected, it's like oh I understand like why rhyme has the same etymology as this other word if you really thought about, but you just like don't think about it, right? There's like this computational explosion. Um, I don't know if that addresses the fact that humans we know can do this, right? Like humans have in fact done this, and I don't know a single example of LLMs ever having done it.
turns.zh: 顺便说一下，这个问题是问 Dario 的：你看，这些模型记住了所有人类知识，你会想如果一个人记住了这么多东西，而且他们还算聪明，他们应该能在不同领域之间建立所有这些联系。而且确实有人类这样做的例子。比如 Donald Swan 之类的人。他注意到镁缺乏后大脑发生的情况与偏头痛时看到的某种结构完全一样。所以他说，好吧，服用镁补充剂，我们就能治愈一批偏头痛，结果确实有效。还有很多类似的例子，人们只是注意到知识之间的两个不同联系。如果这些大语言模型是智能的，为什么它们不能利用这个独特优势做出这类发现呢？嗯，在你们面前回答 AI 问题我有点不好意思。但实际上 Scott Alexander 在他的一个问答帖中回答了这个问题，他说，你看，人类也没有这种逻辑全知，对吧？他举了语言的例子：如果你真的去想为什么两个词有关联，比如为什么 rhyme 和另一个词有相同的词源，如果你真的去想，你会明白，但你就是不会去想，对吧？这存在一种计算爆炸。嗯，我不知道这是否回应了人类确实能做到这一点的事实？人类确实做到了，而我不知道大语言模型有任何一个例子做到了。

ts.sec: Pre-training vs. RL for Novel Discovery

turns.en: What is your answer to this? I think my answer at the moment is that the pre-training objective doesn't necessarily give you this nice, flexible, general knowledge about the world, but doesn't necessarily give you the skill of making novel connections or doing research—the kinds of things that people are trained to do through PhD programs and through the process of exploring and interacting with the world. So I think at a minimum, you need significant RL in at least similar things to be able to approach making novel discoveries. I would like to see some early evidence of this as we start to build models that are interacting with the world and trying to make scientific discoveries, modeling the behaviors that we expect of people in these positions, because I don't think we've done that in a meaningful or scaled way as a field.
turns.zh: 你对这个问题的答案是什么？我目前的答案是，预训练目标不一定能给你这种灵活、通用的世界知识，也不一定能给你做出新颖联系或进行研究的能力——这些是人们在博士项目以及探索和与世界互动的过程中训练出来的。所以我认为，至少你需要大量的强化学习，在类似的事情上，才能接近做出新颖的发现。我希望看到一些早期证据，当我们开始构建与世界互动并试图做出科学发现的模型时，模拟我们对这些位置上的人的期望行为，因为我认为我们作为一个领域还没有以有意义或规模化的方式做到这一点。

turns.en: Yeah, riffing off that with respect to RL, I wonder if models currently just aren't good at knowing what memories they should be storing. Most of their training is just predicting the next word on the internet and remembering very specific facts from that. But if you were to teach me something new right now, I'm very aware of my own memory limitations, so I would try to construct some summary that would stick. Models currently don't have the opportunity to do that memory scaffolding. In general, it's just very primitive right now.
turns.zh: 是的，顺着这个关于强化学习的话题，我想知道模型目前是否不擅长知道应该存储哪些记忆。它们的大部分训练只是预测互联网上的下一个词，并记住其中的非常具体的事实。但如果你现在教我一些新东西，我非常清楚自己的记忆限制，所以我会尝试构建一些能记住的总结。模型目前没有机会做这种记忆支架。总的来说，现在这还很原始。

turns.en: Exactly. Like someone worked on it, it was awesome, it got far, but another excited Anthropic employee then iterated on the memory scaffold and was able to very quickly improve on it. Interesting. So yeah, that's one. I do also just wonder if models are idiot savants. The best analogy might be to Kim Peek. Kim Peek was born without a corpus callosum, if I recall correctly. Each hemisphere of his brain operated quite independently. He could read a page of a book—he'd open a book, there'd be two pages visible, each eye would read one of the pages—and he had a perfect encyclopedic memory of everything he'd ever read. But at the same time, he had other debilitations, functioning socially, these sorts of things. It's just kind of amazing how good LLMs are at very niche topics but can totally fail on others.
turns.zh: 完全正确。比如有人研究过这个，很厉害，进展很大，但另一位兴奋的Anthropic员工随后迭代了记忆支架，并能够非常迅速地改进它。有趣。所以，是的，这是一个。我也确实在想模型是不是白痴天才。最好的类比可能是金·匹克。金·匹克出生时没有胼胝体，如果我没记错的话。他的大脑两个半球相当独立地运作。他能读一页书——他打开一本书，会看到两页，每只眼睛读一页——而且他对读过的一切都有完美的百科全书式记忆。但与此同时，他在社交功能等方面有其他障碍。LLM在非常小众的话题上如此擅长，但在其他话题上完全失败，这真是令人惊讶。

turns.en: I really want to double-click on this thing of why there's this trade-off between memorization. Why is cutting it off apparently connected to this debilitation? But why can't we? WikiText is like 5 megabytes of information. The human brain can store much more. So why does the human brain just not want us to learn and memorize these kinds of things, and is actively pruning? I don't know. We don't know how to do it right now. We'll do a separate episode. One thing I'll say on that is there was another case study of someone with a perfect memory—they never forgot anything—but their memory was too debilitating. It'd be like your context window for the Transformer is trillions of tokens, and then you spend all your time attending to past things and are too trapped in the details to extract any meaningful generalizable insights from it. Terrence Deacon, whose book you recommended, had this interesting insight about how we learn best when we're children, but we forget literally everything that happened to us when we were children—we have total amnesia. Adults have this in-between where we don't remember exact details but we can still learn in a pretty decent way. And then LLMs are on the opposite end of this gradient where they'll get the exact phrasing of WikiText down but won't be able to generalize in these very obvious ways.
turns.zh: 我真的很想深入探讨为什么记忆和泛化之间存在这种权衡。为什么切断记忆显然与这种障碍有关？但我们为什么不能呢？WikiText大约有5兆字节的信息。人脑可以存储更多。那么为什么人脑就是不想让我们学习和记忆这类东西，并且主动修剪呢？我不知道。我们现在不知道怎么做。我们会做单独的一集。关于这一点我要说的是，还有另一个案例研究，一个人有完美的记忆——他从不忘记任何事情——但他的记忆太有障碍了。就像Transformer的上下文窗口有数万亿个token，然后你所有时间都在关注过去的事情，过于陷入细节，无法从中提取任何有意义的可泛化的见解。你推荐的那本书的作者特伦斯·迪肯有一个有趣的见解：我们在儿童时期学习最好，但我们完全忘记了儿童时期发生的一切——我们有完全的失忆。成年人处于中间状态，我们不记得确切的细节，但我们仍然可以以相当不错的方式学习。而LLM处于这个梯度的另一端，它们会记住WikiText的确切措辞，但无法以这些非常明显的方式进行泛化。

turns.en: A little bit like GW's theory? Optimize a theory? No, yeah, yeah. I think I probably got it from that. Yeah, G Gordon has definitely had a big influence on all this.
turns.zh: 有点像GW的理论？优化一个理论？不，是的，是的。我想我可能是从那里得到的。是的，G·戈登肯定对这一切产生了很大影响。

turns.en: I feel like what's underappreciated on the podcast is we have this group chat and we also just meet up a lot in person. All the offer from the podcast just comes from you and a couple other people just feeding me ideas and nudges and whatever, and then I can just use that as an intuition pump during the conversation.
turns.zh: 我觉得在播客中被低估的是，我们有这个群聊，而且我们也经常线下见面。播客中的所有内容都来自你和其他几个人，给我提供想法、推动等等，然后我可以在对话中将其用作直觉泵。

turns.en: You're not the only one. What do you mean? Oh, like I benefit immensely from just hearing what everyone else has to say. And then yeah, it's all regurgitation. In what way? Yeah, yeah.
turns.zh: 你不是唯一一个。你什么意思？哦，就像我从听到其他人的发言中受益匪浅。然后是的，这都是复述。以什么方式？是的，是的。

turns.en: Another question. Yes. Uh, maybe 'rabid monkey' asks: Imagine you have a 17-year-old brother/nephew just starting college. What would you recommend he study given your AGI timelines?
turns.zh: 另一个问题。是的。呃，也许是'rabid monkey'问：想象你有一个17岁的弟弟/侄子刚上大学。根据你的AGI时间线，你会建议他学什么？

turns.en: I don't know. Become a podcaster. I feel like that job's still going to be around. It's funny because I studied computer science and in retrospect, I mean, other time like you know, you could have become a software engineer or something instead. You became a podcaster. It's kind of an irresponsible career move, but in retrospect it's like it kind of worked out just as these guys are getting automated. I get asked this question all the time. Yes. Okay, go. Um, and one answer that I like to give is that you should think about the next couple of years as increasing your individual leverage by a huge factor every year. So you know, already software engineers will come up and say, 'I'm two times faster' or 'in new languages I'm five times faster than I was last year.' I expect that trend line to continue, basically as you sort of go from this model of 'I'm working with some model that's assisting me on my computer and it's like a pairing session' to 'I'm managing a small team' through to 'I'm managing like a division or a company basically that is targeting a task.' So I think that deep technical knowledge in fields will still matter in four years. It absolutely will, because you will be in the position of managing dozens or, like, your individual management bandwidth will be maxed out by trying to manage teams of AIs and this kind of thing. And maybe AIs end up like a truly Singularity world where you have AI managing AI and this kind of stuff, but I think in a very wide part of the possibility spectrum, yes, you are managing enormous, vastly more resources than an individual could command today. And you should be able to solve so many more things with that. That's right. And I think I would emphasize that this is not just cope. It genuinely is the case that these models lack the kind of long-term coherence which is absolutely necessary for making a successful company or just getting an office is kind of complicated, right? You have to deal with all these things. So you can just imagine that for a sector after sector, the economy is really big and really...
turns.zh: 我不知道。成为一名播客主播。我觉得这份工作还会存在。有趣的是，我学了计算机科学，回想起来，我的意思是，其他时候，你知道，你本可以成为一名软件工程师之类的。你成了播客主播。这是一个有点不负责任的职业选择，但回想起来，它似乎正好在这些家伙被自动化的时候成功了。我经常被问到这个问题。是的。好的，开始。嗯，我喜欢给出的一个答案是，你应该把未来几年看作你的个人杠杆每年都在大幅增加。所以你知道，软件工程师已经会过来说，'我快了两倍'或者'在新语言中我比去年快了五倍。'我预计这种趋势会继续，基本上你从'我在和某个模型合作，它在我电脑上协助我，就像结对编程'的模式，到'我在管理一个小团队'，再到'我在管理一个部门或一个公司，基本上针对一个任务。'所以我认为，四年后，领域的深厚技术知识仍然重要。绝对重要，因为你会处于管理数十个的位置，或者，你的个人管理带宽会被试图管理AI团队之类的事情耗尽。也许AI最终会进入一个真正的奇点世界，AI管理AI之类的东西，但我认为在可能性谱系的很大一部分中，是的，你在管理巨大的、比个人今天能指挥的多得多的资源。而且你应该能用这些解决更多事情。没错。而且我想强调，这不是自我安慰。这些模型确实缺乏那种长期连贯性，这对于创办一家成功的公司或仅仅拥有一个办公室来说是绝对必要的，对吧？你必须处理所有这些事情。所以你可以想象，一个又一个行业，经济真的很大，真的……

ts.sec: Career advice and being close to the frontier

turns.en: Complex exactly. And so especially if it's—I mean, I don't know the details, but I assume if it's like a data sparse thing where you kind of just got to know what's actually the context of what's happening in the sector or something, I feel like you'd be in a good position. Maybe the other thought I have is that it's really hard to plan your career in general, and I don't know what advice that implies. Because I remember being super frustrated—I mean, I was in college and the reason I was doing the podcast was to figure out what it is I want to do. It wasn't the podcast itself, and it would go on like 80,000 Hours or whatever career advice, and it's just like in retrospect was all kind of mostly useless. And just like, just try doing things. I mean, especially with AI, we just don't know what it's going to—it's so hard to forecast what kind of transformations there will be. So try things, do things. I mean, it's such vague advice, but I am quite skeptical of career advice in general. Well, maybe the piece of career advice I'm not skeptical of is: put yourself close to the frontier.
turns.zh: 完全正确。尤其是如果——我的意思是，我不知道细节，但假设这是一个数据稀疏的情况，你只需要了解某个领域的实际背景，我觉得你会处于一个有利的位置。另一个想法是，规划职业生涯总体上非常困难，我不知道这暗示了什么建议。因为我记得自己当时非常沮丧——我在大学时做播客就是为了弄清楚自己想做什么。不是播客本身，而是像80,000 Hours之类的职业建议，事后看来大多没什么用。就是，去尝试做事。尤其是AI领域，我们根本不知道未来会怎样——很难预测会有哪些变革。所以去尝试、去做事。我知道这建议很模糊，但我对职业建议总体上持怀疑态度。不过有一条我不怀疑：让自己靠近前沿。

turns.en: Mhm. Because you have a much better vantage point.
turns.zh: 嗯，因为你有更好的视角。

turns.en: That's right. Right. Like you can study deep technical things, whether it's computer science or biology or like, and get to the point where you can see what the issues are. Because it's actually remarkably obvious at the frontier what the problems are, and it's very difficult to see. But actually, do you think there is an opportunity? Because one of the things people bring up is that maybe the people who are advanced in their career and have all this task knowledge will be in a position to be accelerated by AI. But you guys four years ago or two years ago when you were getting discovered or something, that kind of thing where you have a GitHub open issue and you try to solve it—is that just like that's done, and so the onboarding is much harder?
turns.zh: 没错。你可以学习深度的技术内容，无论是计算机科学还是生物学，达到能看清问题的程度。因为在前沿，问题其实非常明显，只是很难看到。但你觉得有机会吗？因为有人提到，那些职业生涯资深、拥有大量任务知识的人可能会被AI加速。但你们四年前或两年前刚被发现时，那种在GitHub上看到开放问题就去解决的方式——是不是已经过时了，所以入门变得更难了？

turns.en: It's still what we look for in hiring. So, you know, yeah. I'm in favor of learning fundamentals, gaining useful mental models. But it feels like everything should be done in an AI-native way, or top-down instead of bottom-up learning. So first of all, learn things more efficiently by using the AI models, and then just know where their capabilities are and aren't.
turns.zh: 这仍然是我们招聘时看重的。所以我赞成学习基础、获得有用的思维模型。但感觉一切都应该以AI原生的方式进行，或者自上而下而不是自下而上地学习。所以首先，通过使用AI模型更高效地学习，然后了解它们的能力边界。

turns.en: Yeah. And I would be worried and skeptical about any subject which prioritizes rote memorization of lots of facts or information.
turns.zh: 是的。我会对任何优先死记硬背大量事实或信息的学科感到担忧和怀疑。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah, yeah. Instead of ways of thinking.
turns.zh: 对，而不是思维方式。

turns.en: Yeah. But if you're always using the AI tools to help you, then you'll naturally just have a good sense for the things that it is and isn't good at.
turns.zh: 是的。但如果你一直使用AI工具来辅助，你自然会对它擅长和不擅长的事情有很好的感觉。

turns.en: That's right. Yeah, yeah, yeah.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Next one: what is your strategy, method, or criteria for choosing guests? The most important thing is: do I want to spend one to two weeks reading every single thing you have ever written, every single interview ever recorded, talking to a bunch of other people about your research? Because I get asked by people who are quite influential often to be like, 'Would you have me on your podcast?' And more often than not, I say no for two reasons. One is just like, okay, you're influential or something, it's just not fundamentally that interesting as an interview prospect. Not from like—I don't think about the hour that I'll spend with you; I think about the two weeks, because this is my life, right? The research is my life, and I want to have fun while doing it. So just like, is this going to be an interesting two weeks to spend? Is it going to help me with my future research or something? And the other is: big guests don't really matter that much. If you just look at one of the most popular episodes or what in the long run helps the podcast grow, by far my most popular guest is Sarah Payne, and she before I interviewed her was just a scholar who was not publicly well-known at all, and I just found her books quite interesting. Same goes with—so my most popular guests are Sarah Payne, and then Sarah Payne, Sarah Payne, because I did a series with her. Awesome. And by the way, from a viewer-minute adjusted basis, I host an S&P podcast where I occasionally talk about AI, and then is David Reich, who is a geneticist of ancient DNA. I mean, he's like somewhat well-known, but he had a bestselling book, but he's not like—he's not Sam Altman or Mark Zuckerberg, who are the next people on the list. And then again, I think at like pretty soon it's like you guys or Leopold or something, and then you get to the lab CEOs or something. So big names just don't matter that much for what I'm actually trying to do. And it's also really hard to predict who's going to be the David Reich or Sarah Payne. So just like, have fun, talk to whoever you want to spend time researching, and it's a pretty good proxy for what will actually be popular.
turns.zh: 下一个问题：你选择嘉宾的策略、方法或标准是什么？最重要的是：我是否愿意花一到两周时间阅读你写过的所有东西、看过的所有采访、和很多人讨论你的研究？因为经常有相当有影响力的人问我：‘你能请我上播客吗？’我通常拒绝，原因有二。第一，你有影响力，但作为采访对象，本质上并不那么有趣。我不是考虑和你相处的那一小时，而是那两周——因为这是我的生活，研究就是我的生活，我想乐在其中。所以，这会不会是值得花的两周？会不会对我未来的研究有帮助？第二，大牌嘉宾其实没那么重要。看看最受欢迎的几期，或者长期帮助播客增长的因素，我最受欢迎的嘉宾是Sarah Payne，她在被我采访前只是一个完全不为人知的学者，我只是觉得她的书很有趣。同样，我最受欢迎的嘉宾就是Sarah Payne，然后还是Sarah Payne，因为我跟她做了一系列节目。顺便说一句，按观众分钟数调整后，我主持一个S&P播客，偶尔聊AI，然后是David Reich，一位古DNA遗传学家。他有点名气，出过畅销书，但他不是Sam Altman或Mark Zuckerberg，那两位是榜单上的下一位。然后很快就是你们或Leopold，然后是实验室CEO之类。所以大牌对我实际想做的事没那么重要。而且很难预测谁会变成David Reich或Sarah Payne。所以，享受过程，和你想花时间研究的人聊天，这很能预示什么内容会真正受欢迎。

ts.sec: Realizing podcast was a viable long-term strategy

turns.en: What was the specific moment, if there was one, that you realized that producing your podcast was a viable long-term strategy? I think when I was shopping around ad spots for the Mark Zuckerberg episode, and which were—you know, like now when I look back on it, it's like not in retrospect that mind-blowing, but at the time I'm like, 'Oh, I could actually hire an editor full-time, or maybe more than one editor.' And from there, turning into a real business. That's when—because before, I just didn't—people would tell me like, 'Oh, these other podcasts are making whatever amount of money,' and I'd be like, 'How?' You know. I have this running joke with one of my friends that I don't know if you've seen me do this, but every time I encounter like a young person who's like, 'What should I do with my life?' I'm like, 'You got to start a blog, you got to be the Matt Levine of AI, you can do this, it's a totally empty niche, you can—' And I have this running joke with them where they're like, 'You're like a country bumpkin who's won the lottery, and you're like, you go to every guy, like, get the scratch pad, get the scratch pad.'
turns.zh: 有没有一个具体的时刻，让你意识到制作播客是一个可行的长期策略？我想是在我为Mark Zuckerberg那期节目寻找广告位的时候。现在回头看，那并不惊人，但当时我想：‘哦，我其实可以全职雇一个编辑，甚至不止一个。’从那以后，它变成了一门真正的生意。因为在那之前，人们告诉我其他播客赚了多少钱，我总在想：‘怎么做到的？’我和一个朋友有个老笑话，不知道你见过没有——每次遇到年轻人问‘我该做什么？’我就说：‘你得开个博客，成为AI界的Matt Levine，你能做到，这完全是个空白领域。’然后他们就说：‘你就像个中了彩票的乡下人，到处跟人说，买刮刮乐，买刮刮乐。’

turns.en: I do want to press on that a bit more, because your immediate answer to the 17-year-old was to start a podcast. So like, what niches are there? What sort of things would you be excited to see in like new blogs, podcasts? I mean, I wonder if you guys think this too, but I think this 'Matt Levine of AI' absolutely is like a totally open niche, as far as I can tell. And I apologize to those who are trying to fill it—I'm aware of at least one that's trying to do this. The other thing I'd really emphasize is it is really hard to do this based on other people's advice, or to say like, 'Here's a niche.' I'm like—I at least I'm trying not to fill a specific niche. And if you think about any sort of successful new media thing out there, it has two things which are true: it's often not just geared towards one particular topic or interest, and two, the most important thing is that it is propelled by a single person's vision.
turns.zh: 我想再追问一下，因为你给17岁孩子的直接建议是开始做播客。那么有哪些细分领域？你希望看到什么样的新博客或播客？我想你们可能也这么想，但我觉得‘AI界的Matt Levine’绝对是一个完全开放的领域，据我所知。我向那些试图填补这个领域的人道歉——我知道至少有一个人在尝试。另一件我想强调的事是，根据别人的建议来做这件事非常困难，或者说‘这里有个细分领域’。我至少试图不去填补某个特定领域。想想任何成功的新媒体，它们都有两个特点：通常不只针对一个特定话题或兴趣；第二，最重要的是，它由一个人的愿景驱动。

turns.en: Not like a collective or whatever. And so I would just really emphasize—sorry, the thing I really want to emphasize is: it can be done, you can make a lot of money at it, which is probably not the most important thing for the kind of person who would succeed at it, but still, it's just worth knowing that it's a viable career. Three: that basically you're going to feel like in the beginning, all your early stuff is going to kind of suck. Maybe some of it will get appreciated, but it seems like bad advice to say still stick through it in case you actually are terrible—because some people are terrible—but in case you are not, just do it. What is three months of vlogging on the side really going to cost you? People just don't actually seriously do the thing for long enough to get evidence or get the sort of RL feedback on how to do it, how to frame an argument, how to make a compelling thing that people want to read or watch.
turns.zh: 不是像集体或什么的。所以我真的想强调——抱歉，我真正想强调的是：这是可以做到的，你可以从中赚很多钱，这对于那种能成功的人来说可能不是最重要的事，但仍然值得知道这是一个可行的职业。第三：基本上你一开始会觉得你所有早期的东西都会有点烂。也许有些会被欣赏，但说“坚持下去，万一你真的很差”似乎是坏建议——因为有些人确实很差——但如果你不差，就去做。三个月的副业vlog真的会花你什么？人们只是没有认真做足够长的时间来获得证据或那种强化学习反馈，关于如何做，如何构建论点，如何做出人们想读或看的引人入胜的东西。

turns.en: Blogging is definitely underrated. I think most of us have—you both had blogs which were relevant, I don't know if relevant to getting that, but they were somewhat relevant. But I think more so that we have all read almost all the blogs that do an in-depth treatise on AI. If you write something that is high quality, it is almost invariably going to be shared around Twitter and Reddit. This is so underappreciated. So two pieces of evidence: I was talking to a very famous blogger, you would know, and I was asking him how often do you discover a new undiscovered blogger, and he was like, yeah, happens very rarely, maybe once a year. And I asked him how long after you discover him or her does the rest of the world discover them, and he was like, maybe a week. That suggests it's actually really efficient.
turns.zh: 写博客绝对被低估了。我认为我们大多数人都有——你们俩都有相关的博客，我不知道是否对成功有直接帮助，但它们是有些相关的。但我更认为我们都读过几乎所有深入探讨AI的博客。如果你写了一些高质量的东西，它几乎肯定会出现在Twitter和Reddit上被分享。这太被低估了。所以有两个证据：我和一个非常有名的博主聊过，你们会知道是谁，我问他你多久发现一个新的未被发现的博主，他说，嗯，很少发生，大概一年一次。我又问他你发现他或她之后多久世界其他地方会发现他们，他说，大概一周。这说明它实际上非常高效。

ts.sec: Slow Compounding Growth vs. Viral Hits

turns.en: I believe that slow compounding growth in media is kind of fake. Like Leopold's situation or others, it's not like he was building up an audience for a long time for years or something; it just was really good. Disagree or agree with it? If it's good enough, literally everybody who matters—and I mean that literally—will read it. I think it's hard to zero-shot something like that, but the fundamental thing to emphasize is the compounding growth, at least for me, has been that I feel like I've gotten better. It's not so much that the three years of having 1,000 followers was somehow compounding; I don't think it was that. I think it was just that it took a while to get better. Suddenly when Leopold posted that, the next day it was almost like you could picture it being stapled to the wall on Twitter, everyone was talking about it. You went to any event for the following week, every single person in the entire city was talking about that essay. Like Renaissance Florence, that's right.
turns.zh: 我认为媒体中的缓慢复利增长是有点假的。就像Leopold的情况或其他，并不是说他多年来一直在积累观众；它只是非常好。你同意还是不同意？如果它足够好，字面上所有重要的人——我是字面意思——都会读到它。我认为很难零次尝试就做到那样，但根本要强调的是，复利增长，至少对我来说，是我觉得自己变好了。并不是说拥有1000粉丝的三年是某种复利；我不认为是那样。我认为只是花了一段时间才变好。突然当Leopold发了那篇，第二天几乎就像你能想象它被钉在Twitter的墙上，每个人都在谈论它。接下来的一周你去任何活动，整个城市的每个人都在谈论那篇文章。就像文艺复兴时期的佛罗伦萨，没错。

ts.sec: First Big Success and the Power of Articulation

turns.en: What would you say was your first big success? I'm trying to think back to when I first found your podcast. I distinctly remember you had your blog post on the Anis Marabouts and Jeff Bezos retweeted it, I think. I'm trying to remember if it was even before that or not. But yeah, I'm curious.
turns.zh: 你觉得你的第一个大成功是什么？我试着回想我第一次发现你的播客的时候。我清楚地记得你有一篇关于Anis Marabouts的博客文章，杰夫·贝佐斯转发了它，我想。我试着回忆是否在那之前还有别的。但没错，我很好奇。

turns.en: Yeah, okay. I mean, it wasn't something where I'm like, it was some big insight that deserved to blow up like that. It was just taking some shots on goal. They were all like whatever, insight-porny, and then one of them caught the right guy's attention. But I think that was it. That's something else we just underappreciate: a piece of writing doesn't need to have a fundamentally new insight so much as give people a way to express cleanly a set of ideas that they are already aware of in a broader way. If it's really crisp and articulate, then even still that's very valuable.
turns.zh: 是的，好吧。我的意思是，这并不是什么了不起的洞见，值得那样爆红。只是碰碰运气。它们都像是那种“洞见色情”，然后其中一个引起了正确的人注意。但我想就是这样。这是另一件我们低估的事：一篇文章并不需要有根本性的新洞见，而是给人们一种方式，让他们干净地表达他们已经以更广泛方式意识到的一系列想法。如果它非常清晰和有条理，那仍然非常有价值。

ts.sec: The True Feedback Loop: Meeting People

turns.en: The one thing I should emphasize, which I think is maybe the most important thing, is the feedback loop. It's not the compounding growth of the audience. I don't even think it's the compounding like me getting more shots on goal in terms of doing the podcast. I actually don't think you improve that much by just doing the same thing again and again if there's no reward signal; you'll just keep doing whatever you were doing before. I genuinely think the most important thing has been that the podcast is good enough that it merits me getting to meet people like you guys. Then I become friends with people like you, you guys teach me stuff, I produce more good podcasts hopefully slightly better, that helps me meet people in other fields, they teach me more things. Like with the China thing recently, I wrote this blog post about a couple stories about things that happened in China, and that alone has netted me an amazing China network in the matter of one blog post. So hopefully if I do an episode on China, it will be better as a result. And hopefully that happens across field after field. So just getting to meet people like you is actually the main sort of flywheel.
turns.zh: 我要强调的一件事，我认为可能是最重要的，就是反馈循环。这不是观众的复利增长。我甚至不认为这是我在做播客时获得更多尝试机会的复利。我实际上不认为如果你只是重复做同样的事情而没有奖励信号，你会进步很多；你会继续做你之前做的事。我真心认为最重要的是播客足够好，让我有机会遇到像你们这样的人。然后我和像你们这样的人成为朋友，你们教我东西，我制作更多好的播客，希望稍微更好，这帮助我遇到其他领域的人，他们教我更多东西。就像最近关于中国的事，我写了一篇博客文章，讲了几件在中国发生的事，仅这一篇文章就让我获得了一个惊人的中国网络。所以希望如果我做一期关于中国的节目，它会因此更好。希望这能一个领域接一个领域地发生。所以遇到像你们这样的人实际上是主要的飞轮。

ts.sec: Moving to San Francisco and Bench Press

turns.en: Interesting. So move to San Francisco? Yes, if you're trying to do AI. Yeah.
turns.zh: 有趣。所以搬到旧金山？是的，如果你想做AI的话。

turns.en: Next questions. Shall we do? We can do. Oh, very important question from Jack Pajit: how much can you bench? You can't lie, because we...
turns.zh: 下一个问题。我们开始吧？可以。哦，来自Jack Pajit的非常重要的问题：你能卧推多少？你不能撒谎，因为我们……

turns.en: At one point I did bench 225. Actually, I think I benched 225 for 4 reps. Now I think I'm probably like 20 pounds lighter than that or something. The reason you guys are asking me this is because I've gone lifting with both of you, and I remember Trent and I were doing pull-ups and bench, and then we like bench, and he'd like throw on another plate or something, and then instead of pull-ups he'd be cranking out these muscle-ups. It's all technique. Yeah, so they both bench more than me, but I'm trying my best. Ask again in six months.
turns.zh: 有一次我卧推了225磅。实际上，我想我卧推225磅做了4次。现在我想我大概比那时轻了20磅左右。你们问我这个是因为我和你们俩都一起举过铁，我记得Trent和我做引体向上和卧推，然后我们卧推，他会再加一片杠铃片什么的，然后不做引体向上，而是做肌肉引体向上。这都是技巧。是的，所以他们俩卧推都比我多，但我在尽力。六个月后再问吧。

ts.sec: Favorite History Book and LBJ Quote

turns.en: What's your favorite history book? There's a wall of them behind you.
turns.zh: 你最喜欢的历史书是什么？你身后有一墙的书。

turns.en: Oh, obviously the Robert Caro LBJ biographies. Yeah, the main thing I took away from those books is LBJ had this quote that he would tell his debate school students in his early 20s. He taught debate to these poor Mexican students in Texas, and he used to tell them: if you do everything, you'll win. I think it's an underrated quote. So that's the main thing I took away. And you see it through his entire career: there's a reasonable amount of effort which goes by like 80/20; you do the 20 to get 80% of the effect, and then you go beyond that to get like, oh no, I'm not just going to do the 20%, I'm going to just like do the whole thing. And there's a level even beyond that, which is like this is just like reasonable use of time, this is going to have no ultimate impact, and still try doing that.
turns.zh: 哦，显然是罗伯特·卡罗的LBJ传记。是的，我从那些书中学到的主要是LBJ有一个他20岁出头时对辩论班学生说的话。他在德克萨斯教那些贫穷的墨西哥学生辩论，他常告诉他们：如果你做所有事，你就会赢。我认为这是一句被低估的名言。所以这是我学到的主要东西。你在他整个职业生涯中都能看到：有一个合理的努力程度，就像80/20法则；你付出20%得到80%的效果，然后你超越它，得到像，哦不，我不只是做20%，我要做全部。甚至还有一个层次，就是这仅仅是合理利用时间，这不会有最终影响，但仍然尝试去做。

turns.en: Yeah, you've shared on Twitter using Anki, even like a claw integration. Yeah, do you do book clubs? Do you use Goodreads? And what are you reading right now?
turns.zh: 是啊，你在推特上分享过用 Anki，甚至还有那种 Claw 集成。你参加读书俱乐部吗？用 Goodreads 吗？你现在在读什么？

turns.en: I don't use book clubs. I do have the Space Edition, which has genuinely been a huge uplift in my ability to learn. Mostly because it's not even the long-term impact over years, though I think that is part of it. And I do regret all the episodes I did without using space cards because all the insights have just sort of faded away. The main thing is, if you're studying a complicated subject, at least for me, it's been super helpful to consolidate. It's like if you don't do it, you feel like a general who says, 'I'm going to wage a campaign against this country,' and then you climb one hill, and the next day you have to retreat, and then you climb the same hill. There might be a more kosher analogy. The other question was what am I reading right now. Yeah, my friend Alvaro de Menard, author of fantastic 'Anacronismo', made like a hundred copies of this translation he did of his favorite Greek poet. They're like, yeah, K.P. Kavafy — hopefully I didn't mispronounce it. That one has an inscription for G because that's his coffee, but it's super delightful, and that's what I've been reading recently. Any insights from it so far? Poets will hate this framing. I feel like poetry is like TikTok, where you get this quick vibe of a certain thing and then you like swipe and then you get the next vibe, swipe.
turns.zh: 我不参加读书俱乐部。但我有 Space Edition，它确实极大地提升了我学习的能力。主要不是因为多年的长期影响——虽然那也是部分原因——我后悔那些没用空间卡做的节目，因为那些见解都淡忘了。关键是，如果你在研究一个复杂的课题，至少对我来说，它非常有助于巩固。就像如果你不做，你会感觉自己像个将军，说‘我要对这个国家发动一场战役’，然后你爬上一座山，第二天又得撤退，然后又爬同一座山。可能有个更恰当的比喻。另一个问题是现在在读什么。我朋友阿尔瓦罗·德·梅纳德，写了很棒的《Anacronismo》，他把自己最喜欢的希腊诗人的译本做了一百本。是卡瓦菲——希望我没读错。那本上面有给 G 的题词，因为那是他的咖啡，但读起来非常愉快，这就是我最近在读的。目前有什么见解吗？诗人会讨厌这个比喻。我觉得诗歌就像 TikTok，你得到某种东西的快速氛围，然后你滑动，得到下一个氛围，再滑动。

turns.en: How do you go about learning new things or preparing for an episode? Like what does that look like? You mentioned the one to two week period where you're deep diving on the person. What does that actually look like?
turns.zh: 你是怎么学习新东西或准备节目的？比如那是什么样的？你提到有一到两周的时间你会深入研究那个人。实际上是什么样的？

turns.en: It's very much the obvious thing: you read their books, if they have papers you read the papers, if they have colleagues you try to talk to them to better understand the field. I will also mention that all I have to do is ask some questions. And I do think it's much harder to learn a field to be a practitioner than just learn enough to ask interesting questions. But yeah, for that, it's very much the obvious thing you'd expect.
turns.zh: 这很明显：你读他们的书，如果有论文就读论文，如果有同事就试着和他们聊聊，以更好地理解这个领域。我还要说，我只需要问一些问题。而且我认为，要成为一个领域的实践者比仅仅学足够多来问有趣的问题要难得多。但没错，就这一点而言，就是你会预料到的那些显而易见的事情。

turns.en: Based on Carl Sean's question: what are your long-term goals and ambitions?
turns.zh: 基于卡尔·肖恩的问题：你的长期目标和抱负是什么？

turns.en: Yeah, the AGI kind of makes the prospect of a long term harder to articulate, right? You know the Peter Thiel quote about 'what is your 10-year plan and why can't you do it in 6 months?' It's especially salient with good timelines. For the foreseeable future, grow the podcast and do more episodes, maybe more writing. But yeah, we'll see what happens after like 10 years or something. The world might be different enough. So basically podcast for now.
turns.zh: 是啊，AGI 让长期前景更难说清楚了，对吧？你知道彼得·蒂尔的那句话：‘你的十年计划是什么，为什么不能在六个月内完成？’在好的时间线下尤其贴切。在可预见的未来，发展播客，做更多节目，可能多写点东西。但没错，十年后之类的再看吧。世界可能已经大不相同。所以目前基本就是播客。

turns.en: Something you've spoken to me about, particularly when you were trying to hire people for the podcast, was what you wanted to achieve with the podcast. In what way do you want the podcast to shape the world? Do you have any thoughts on that? Because I remember you talking like, 'I really want people to actually understand AI and how this might change their lives, or what we could be doing now to shape the world such that it ends up better.'
turns.zh: 你跟我谈过，特别是在你为播客招人的时候，你想通过播客实现什么。你希望播客以什么方式塑造世界？你有什么想法吗？因为我记得你说过：‘我真的希望人们真正理解 AI，以及这可能会如何改变他们的生活，或者我们现在可以做什么来塑造世界，让它变得更好。’

turns.en: Yeah, I don't know. I have contradictory views on this. On the one end, I do know that important decisions are being made right now in AI, and I do think — riffing on what we're saying about situation awareness — if you do something really good, it has a very high probability of one-shotting the relevant person. People are generally reasonable; if you make a good argument, it'll go places. On the other hand, I just think it's very hard to know what should be done. You have to have the very correct world model, and then you have to know how in that world model the action you're taking is going to have the effect you anticipate. Even in the last week, I've changed my mind on some pretty fundamental things about what I think about the possibility of an intelligence explosion or transformative AI as a result of talking to the Epoch folks. Yeah, basically the TL;DR is I want the podcast to just be an epistemic tool for now, until I — because I think it's just very easy to be wrong. So just having a background level of understanding of the relevant arguments is the highest priority.
turns.zh: 是啊，我不知道。我对此有矛盾的观点。一方面，我知道现在 AI 领域正在做出重要决策，而且我认为——顺着我们说的情境意识——如果你做得非常好，它很有可能一次性打动相关的人。人们通常讲道理；如果你提出好的论点，它会有影响力。另一方面，我只是觉得很难知道应该做什么。你必须拥有非常正确的世界模型，然后你必须知道在那个世界模型中，你采取的行动会如何产生你预期的效果。就在上周，因为和 Epoch 的人交谈，我对一些关于智能爆炸或变革性 AI 可能性的根本性事情改变了看法。基本上，TL;DR 是，我希望播客现在只是一个认知工具，直到我——因为我认为很容易犯错。所以，对相关论点有背景层面的理解是最高优先级。

turns.en: Makes sense. Yeah. What's your sense? What should I be doing? I mean, I think the podcast is awesome, and a lot more people should listen to it, and there are a lot more guests I'd be excited for you to interview. G.L. pretty good answer for now. Yeah, I think making sure that there is a great debate of ideas on not just AI but on other fields and everything is incredibly high leverage and value. Yeah, yeah.
turns.zh: 有道理。是啊。你觉得呢？我应该做什么？我是说，我觉得播客很棒，应该有更多人听，还有很多嘉宾我很期待你去采访。G.L. 现在是个不错的答案。是啊，我认为确保不仅有关于 AI 的，还有关于其他领域和一切的思想大辩论，是极其高杠杆和价值的。是啊，是啊。

turns.en: How do you groom your beard? It's majestic.
turns.zh: 你是怎么打理胡子的？太帅了。

turns.en: I don't know what to say. Just genetics. I do trim it, but no beard oil. I sometimes do beard oil. How often? Once every couple of days. Okay, that's not sometimes, that's pretty often. Do you have different shampoo for your head and your beard? No. What kind of shampoo do you use? Anti-dandruff. Do you condition? Yeah. How often do you push up? We're giving people the answers that they want. Big beard oil. Yeah, you can sell some ad slots to different shampoo companies, and we can edit it. Maybe we sold an ad slot. Sorry.
turns.zh: 我不知道该说什么。就是基因。我会修剪，但不用胡须油。我有时会用胡须油。多久一次？每两天一次。好吧，那不算有时，那是相当频繁。你的头和胡子用不同的洗发水吗？不。你用哪种洗发水？去屑的。你用护发素吗？用。你多久推一次？我们在给人们他们想要的答案。大胡须油。是啊，你可以把广告位卖给不同的洗发水公司，我们可以编辑进去。也许我们已经卖了一个广告位。抱歉。

turns.en: You had this idea of merch. Do you want to explain this idea?
turns.zh: 你有个周边商品的想法。你想解释一下这个想法吗？

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. So people should react to this. Someone should make it happen. D wants merch but he doesn't want to admit that he wants it, or he doesn't want to make it himself because that seems tacky. Yeah, so I really want a plain white tee with just Dwarkesh's beard in the center of it. That's it, nothing else. But you were saying it should have a different texture than the rest of the shirt. Oh, so then I'm ripping off it. Where maybe a limited edition set can have some of your beard hair actually. So that'd be pretty cool. I would pay for that. Okay, how much? I've got patches all over my beard. Depends on how much hair. If it's like one is in there somewhere versus the whole thing. Like do I have to dry clean it? Can I wash it on the delicate setting? But really, I think you should get merch. If you want to grow the podcast, which apparently you do, then one way is beard.
turns.zh: 是啊，是啊。所以人们应该对此做出反应。应该有人来实现它。D 想要周边，但他不想承认他想要，或者他不想自己做，因为那看起来俗气。是啊，所以我真的很想要一件纯白 T 恤，中间只有 Dwarkesh 的胡子。就这样，没别的。但你说它应该和衬衫其他部分有不同的质感。哦，那我是在抄袭它。也许限量版可以放一些你的胡子。那会很酷。我会为此付钱。好的，多少钱？我胡子上有斑块。取决于有多少头发。如果只是一根在里面，还是整个。比如我需要干洗吗？可以用轻柔模式洗吗？但说真的，我觉得你应该做周边。如果你想发展播客，显然你想，那么一种方式就是胡子。

turns.en: Which historical figure would be best suited to run a frontier AI lab? This is definitely a question for you guys. Oh no, I mean I'm curious what your take is first. You've spoken to more of the heads of AI labs than I have. I was going to say LBJ. So the question is: who would be best at running an AI lab, or who would be best for the world, or what outcome do you want? What do you want? Because I imagine it seems like what the best AI lab CEO succeeds at is raising money, building up hype, setting a coherent vision. I don't know how much it matters for the CEO themselves.
turns.zh: 哪个历史人物最适合运营前沿 AI 实验室？这绝对是问你们的问题。哦不，我是说我想先听听你的看法。你和 AI 实验室的负责人谈得比我多。我本来想说 LBJ。所以问题是：谁最擅长运营 AI 实验室，或者谁对世界最好，或者你想要什么结果？你想要什么？因为我想，最好的 AI 实验室 CEO 成功之处在于筹集资金、制造热度、设定连贯的愿景。我不知道这对 CEO 本人有多重要。

turns.en: To have good research taste or something, but it seems like their role is more as a sort of emissary to the rest of the world. And I feel like LBJ would be pretty good at this, like just getting the right concessions, making projects move along, coordinating among different groups. Maybe Robert Moses? Again, not necessarily best for the world, but just in terms of making things happen.
turns.zh: 要有好的研究品味之类的，但他们的角色似乎更像是向世界其他地方的使者。我觉得林登·约翰逊在这方面会很擅长，比如争取正确的让步、推动项目进展、协调不同群体。也许罗伯特·摩西？同样，不一定对世界最好，但就推动事情发生而言。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, I think 'best for the world' is a pretty important precondition.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为“对世界最好”是一个相当重要的前提条件。

turns.en: Oh right. There's a Lord Acton quote: 'Great people are very rarely good people.' So it's hard to think of a great person in history who I feel like would really move the ball forward and also I trust their moral judgment. We're lucky in many senses with the set today, right? Like the set of people today both try and care a lot about the moral side as well as drive the labs forward. This is also why I'm skeptical of big grand schemes like nationalization or some public-private partnership, or just generally shaking up the landscape too much. Because I do think we're in one of the better universes. The difficulty of whether it's alignment or some kind of deployment safety risks is just the nature of the universe making it some level of difficulty. But the human factors in a lot of the counterfactual universes—I feel like we don't end up with people like this. We could be in a universe where they don't even pay lip service. This is not an idea that anybody had. You could have an ASI takeover. I think we live in a pretty good counterfactual universe, a good set of game plays on the board.
turns.zh: 哦对。阿克顿勋爵有句名言：“伟人很少是好人。”所以很难想到历史上哪个伟人既真正推动进步，又让我信任他们的道德判断。在很多意义上，我们很幸运有今天这批人，对吧？今天这批人既努力关心道德层面，又推动实验室前进。这也是为什么我对国有化或公私合作等宏大计划持怀疑态度，或者一般性地过度改变格局。因为我确实认为我们处于一个较好的宇宙中。无论是对齐问题还是部署安全风险，其难度只是宇宙本性带来的某种难度。但在许多反事实宇宙中的人类因素——我觉得我们不会遇到这样的人。我们可能处于一个他们甚至不口头上承认的宇宙。这根本不是任何人想到过的想法。你可能会有ASI接管。我认为我们生活在一个相当好的反事实宇宙中，棋盘上有一手好棋。

turns.en: That's right.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: How are you preparing for fast timelines? If there's fast timelines, then there will be this six-month period in which the most important decisions in human history are being made. And I feel like having an AI podcast during that time might be useful. That's basically the plan.
turns.zh: 你如何为快速时间线做准备？如果时间线很快，那么将会有六个月的时间，人类历史上最重要的决定正在被做出。我觉得在那段时间里做一个AI播客可能会有用。这基本上就是计划。

turns.en: Have you made any shorter-term decisions with regards to spending or health or anything else?
turns.zh: 你有没有在支出、健康或其他方面做出任何短期决定？

turns.en: After I interviewed Zuckerberg, my business bank balance was negative 23 cents. When the ad money hit, I immediately reinvested in Nvidia. So that is the... sorry, but you were asking from a sort of altruistic perspective?
turns.zh: 在我采访扎克伯格之后，我的企业银行余额是负23美分。广告收入到账后，我立即重新投资了英伟达。所以这就是……抱歉，但你是从某种利他主义的角度问的吗？

turns.en: No, no, just in general. Like, have you changed the way you live at all because of your AGI timelines?
turns.zh: 不，不，只是泛泛地问。比如，你有没有因为AGI时间线而改变生活方式？

turns.en: I never looked into getting a Roth IRA. He brought us Fiji Water before, which is in plastic bottles. Changed what?
turns.zh: 我从来没考虑过开罗斯个人退休账户。他之前给我们带过斐济水，是塑料瓶装的。改变了什么？

turns.en: Have you guys changed your lifestyle as a result?
turns.zh: 你们有没有因此改变生活方式？

turns.en: Not really, no. I just like work all the time.
turns.zh: 并没有。我就是一直工作。

turns.en: Would you be doing that anyways, or would you not?
turns.zh: 你本来就会这样，还是不会？

turns.en: Ah, I would probably be going very intensely at whatever thing I'd picked to devote myself to.
turns.zh: 啊，我可能会非常投入地做我选择投身的事情。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. How about you?
turns.zh: 是啊，是啊。你呢？

turns.en: I canceled my 401k contribution. Really, yeah. That felt like a more serious one. It's hard for me to imagine a world in which I have all this money sitting in an account waiting till I'm 60, and things look so different then. But you could be a trillionaire with your marginal 401k contribution, I guess. But you also invest it in specific things, and I don't know, I might change my mind in the future and can restart it. And I've been contributing for a few years now. On a more serious note, one thing I have been thinking about is how could you use this money to an altruistic end. Basically, if there's somebody who's up and coming in the field that I know, which is making content, could I use money to support them? And I'm of two minds on this one. There are people who did this for me, and it was actually responsible for me continuing to do the podcast when it just did not make sense, when there were like a couple hundred people listening or something. Want to shout out Anil Varasi for doing this, and also Leopold actually for the foundation he was previously running. On the other hand, I feel like I would... it's the thing about what that blogger was saying: the good ones you actually do notice. It's like it's hard to find a hidden talent. Maybe I'm totally wrong about this, but I feel like if I put up a sort of grant application—'I give you money if you're trying to make a blog'—I'm actually not sure about how well that would work. There's different things you could do though, like I'll give you money to move to San Francisco for two months and meet people and get more context and taste, feedback on what you're doing. It's not so much about the money or time; it's putting them in an environment where they can more rapidly grow. That's something one could do.
turns.zh: 我取消了401k缴款。真的，是的。那感觉是一个更严肃的决定。我很难想象一个世界，我有一大笔钱躺在账户里等到60岁，而那时情况已经大不相同。但我想，你也许能用你那微不足道的401k缴款成为万亿富翁。不过你也可以投资具体的东西，而且我不知道，我将来可能会改变主意，可以重新开始。我已经缴了好几年了。更严肃地说，我一直在思考的一件事是如何用这笔钱达到利他目的。基本上，如果我知道这个领域有某个崭露头角的人，正在做内容，我能不能用钱支持他们？我对此有两种想法。有人为我做过这件事，这实际上是我继续做播客的原因，当时只有几百人在听之类的。想感谢Anil Varasi，还有Leopold，他之前运营的基金会。另一方面，我觉得我会……就像那个博主说的：真正优秀的人你其实能注意到。很难找到隐藏的人才。也许我完全错了，但我觉得如果我发布一个资助申请——‘如果你尝试做博客，我给你钱’——我不确定效果会如何。不过你可以做不同的事情，比如我给你钱搬到旧金山两个月，认识人，获得更多背景和品味，对你正在做的事情得到反馈。这与其说是钱或时间的问题，不如说是把他们放在一个能更快成长的环境中。这是可以做到的。

turns.en: I mean, you also do that quite proactively in terms of you deliberately introduce people that you think will be interesting to each other and this kind of stuff.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，你也相当主动地做这件事，比如你有意介绍你认为彼此会有趣的人认识，诸如此类。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah, no, I mean that's very fair. And I obviously have benefited a ton from moving to San Francisco. It's unlikely that I would be doing the podcast, at least on AI to the degree I am, if I wasn't here. So maybe it's a mistake to judge people based on the quality of their content as it exists now, and just throw money at them—not throw money, but give them enough money to move to SF to get caught up in this intellectual milieu, and then maybe do something interesting as a result.
turns.zh: 是的，是的，不，我是说这很公平。我显然从搬到旧金山中受益良多。如果我不在这里，我不太可能做播客，至少不会像现在这样深入AI领域。所以也许根据他们现有内容的质量来判断人是错误的，直接给他们钱——不是扔钱，而是给他们足够的钱搬到旧金山，融入这个知识氛围，然后也许因此做出有趣的事情。

turns.en: The thing that most readily comes to mind is the MATS program for AI research. And this seems like it's just been incredibly successful at giving people the time, the funding, and the social status justification to do AI safety relevant research with mentors.
turns.zh: 最容易想到的是AI研究的MATS项目。这个项目似乎非常成功，它给了人们时间、资金和社会地位上的合理性，让他们与导师一起做AI安全相关的研究。

turns.en: Oh, and you have a similar program—we have the Anthropic Fellows program, right?
turns.zh: 哦，你也有一个类似的项目——我们有Anthropic研究员项目，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah. And what is your... I know you're probably selecting for a slightly different thing, but I assume it's going to be power-law dominated. And have you noticed a pattern among the MATS fellows or your fellows who is just like, 'This made the whole thing worth it'? What characteristic?
turns.zh: 是的。你的……我知道你可能在筛选略有不同的东西，但我假设它会遵循幂律分布。你有没有注意到MATS研究员或你的研究员中，有人觉得‘这让整个事情都值得了’？有什么特征？

turns.en: I mean, there have been multiple people who Anthropic and other labs have hired out of this program. So I think the return on investment for it has been massive. And yeah, apparently the fellows—I think there are 20 of them—are like really good. What is the trick to making it work well, or finding that one person?
turns.zh: 我的意思是，Anthropic和其他实验室已经从该项目中雇佣了多人。所以我认为它的投资回报是巨大的。是的，显然这些研究员——我想有20个——都非常优秀。让项目运作良好或找到那个人的诀窍是什么？

turns.en: I think it's gotten much better with time. The early fellows—some of them did good work and got good jobs—so then later fellows, the quality bar has just risen and risen and risen. And there are even better mentors now than before. So it's this really cool flywheel effect. But originally it was just people who didn't have the funding or time to make a name for themselves or do ambitious work. So it's kind of like giving them that niche to do it.
turns.zh: 我认为随着时间的推移，它变得好多了。早期的研究员——其中一些人做了很好的工作并找到了好工作——所以后来的研究员，质量门槛一直在提高。而且现在的导师比以前更好。所以这是一个非常酷的飞轮效应。但最初只是那些没有资金或时间为自己扬名或做雄心勃勃工作的人。所以这有点像给他们那个空间去做。

turns.en: Right, right, right. Seems really key.
turns.zh: 对，对，对。看起来非常关键。

turns.en: Yeah. You can do other things that doesn't have to be money, you know? Like you could put out ideas for things you'd...
turns.zh: 是的。你可以做其他事情，不一定是钱，你知道吧？比如你可以提出一些想法……

turns.en: I'd be really interested in reading that. That's right, like promoting, right? Yeah, yeah, yeah. There's something coming there. Okay, so if this episode hopefully will launch Tuesday at the same time as the book—by the way, which you can get at stripe.press/scaling—but on Wednesday, which is the day after, hopefully there's something useful for you here. Okay, exciting. Yeah, any other questions we want to ask?
turns.zh: 我真的很想读读那个。没错，就像推广，对吧？嗯嗯嗯。有些东西要来了。好的，所以如果这期节目能在周二上线，正好和书同步——顺便说一句，你可以在 stripe.press/scaling 买到——但周三，也就是第二天，希望这里有些对你有用的内容。好的，激动人心。是啊，我们还有什么想问的吗？

turns.en: The thing I have takes on which I rarely get asked about is distribution. Distribution, yeah. No, no, sorry, like MrBeast distribution. Oh yeah, yeah, yeah. Where people, I think rightly, focus on the content. And if that's not up to snuff, I think you won't succeed. But to the extent that somebody's trying to do similar things, the thing they consistently underrate is putting the time into getting distribution right. I just take random takes about, for example, the most successful thing for my podcast in terms of growth has been YouTube Shorts. It's a thing you would never have predicted beforehand. And they're responsible for basically at least half the growth of the podcast or something.
turns.zh: 我很少被问及的一个话题是分发。分发，对。不，不，抱歉，就像 MrBeast 的分发。哦，对对对。人们，我认为正确地，关注内容。如果内容不够好，我认为你不会成功。但就某人试图做类似事情而言，他们一直低估的是花时间把分发做好。我随便举个例子，比如我的播客在增长方面最成功的东西是 YouTube Shorts。这是你事先永远无法预测的事情。它们基本上贡献了播客至少一半的增长。

turns.en: I mean, I buy that. Yeah, why would you predict it? I mean, I guess there's the contrast of the long-form deep content and YouTube Shorts and stuff, but I definitely think the good hooks content, yeah.
turns.zh: 我信这个。是啊，你为什么会预测到呢？我是说，我猜是长深度内容和 YouTube Shorts 之类的对比，但我绝对认为好的钩子内容，对。

turns.en: And I like takes on how to write tweets and stuff. The main intuition being: write like you're writing to a group chat. To a group chat to your friends, rather than this formal whatever. I don't know, just these sorts of things.
turns.zh: 我还喜欢关于如何写推文之类的观点。主要直觉是：写得像你在给群聊发消息。给朋友的群聊，而不是这种正式的什么。我不知道，就是这类东西。

turns.en: Yeah, no, I mean, what else comes to mind here? Maybe it's interesting, the difference between TikTok and YouTube Shorts. Oh yeah, we've never cracked TikTok. Yeah, why not? Like you've tried?
turns.zh: 是啊，不，我是说，还有什么想到的吗？也许有趣的是 TikTok 和 YouTube Shorts 的区别。哦对，我们从来没搞定 TikTok。是啊，为什么？你试过吗？

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, have you done everything? I read these poems. Maybe you're in a bubble bath with some beard shampoo on, reading poems. That would be incredible. I bet you that would go viral. You have to do that now. Reading a poem. Uncross your legs.
turns.zh: 是啊，我是说，你什么都试过了吗？我读这些诗。也许你泡在泡泡浴里，涂着胡须洗发水，读诗。那会不可思议。我打赌那会火。你现在就得做。读一首诗。别跷腿。

turns.en: Last episode it was the interpretability challenge, now it's DoorDash and a bubble bath. I got to sell the book somehow. You know, we literally do it like Margot Robbie explaining the CD. So what is scaling? And that's how you crack distribution. That's... oh, but yeah, no, like when we did our episode, it launched and you were sharing interesting tidbits about how it was doing and the thumbnail you wanted to use and the title. And I think I even asked you to share more details because it seemed interesting and cool and subtle things. But it seemed like you also kind of just hated it, like playing this game of really having to optimize all these knobs.
turns.zh: 上期节目是可解释性挑战，现在是 DoorDash 和泡泡浴。我总得想办法卖书。你知道，我们就像玛格特·罗比解释 CD 那样做。那么什么是 Scaling？这就是你搞定分发的方法。那是……哦，但是，对，不，就像我们做那期节目时，它上线了，你分享了一些有趣的细节，比如表现如何，你想用的缩略图和标题。我想我甚至让你分享更多细节，因为看起来有趣、酷又微妙。但看起来你也有点讨厌它，就像玩这个必须优化所有旋钮的游戏。

turns.en: So what I realized: talent is everything. So having—I'm really lucky to have three to four editors who I'm incredibly proud to work with. I don't know how to hire more of them. They're just so good and self-directed. So honestly, I don't have tips on how to replicate that. I hired those guys. One of them was a farmer in Argentina, one of them was a freshman Master's student in Sri Lanka, one of them was a former editor for one of MrBeast's channels, the other is a director in Czechoslovakia who makes these AI animations that you've seen in the Notes on China and he's working on more essays like that. So I don't know how to replicate that catch again. God, that's a pretty widely... I'm going to be hesitant, damn. But they're all like... God damn. And this was just through your challenges and just tweeting about that? That's right, yeah. I had a competition to make clips of my podcast. I rounded up a couple of them this way. Yeah, it's hard to replicate because I tried.
turns.zh: 所以我意识到：天赋就是一切。所以拥有——我很幸运有三到四位编辑，和他们合作我感到无比自豪。我不知道怎么再招到更多这样的人。他们就是那么优秀，自我驱动。所以老实说，我没有如何复制的建议。我雇了那些人。其中一个曾是阿根廷的农民，一个是斯里兰卡的研究生新生，一个是 MrBeast 某个频道的前编辑，另一个是捷克斯洛伐克的导演，他制作了你在《中国笔记》中看到的那些 AI 动画，他还在做更多那样的文章。所以我不知道怎么再抓到那样的运气。天哪，这相当广泛……我会犹豫，该死。但他们都是……天哪。而这就是通过你的挑战和发推文找到的？没错，对。我举办了一个比赛，为我的播客制作片段。我通过这种方式找到了几个。是啊，很难复制，因为我试过。

turns.en: Why do you think this works so well with the video editors? Because you tried a similar approach with your Chief of Staff.
turns.zh: 你为什么认为这对视频编辑这么有效？因为你对你的幕僚长也试过类似的方法。

turns.en: The difference is with the video editor, I think there is this arbitrage opportunity where there are people—it is fundamentally a sort of 'are you willing to work hard and obsess about getting better over time?'—which all of them go above and beyond on. But you can just find people in other countries who are like... and it's not even about the wages. I've 10x their salaries or something like that. It's just about getting somebody who is really detail-oriented, and there is this global arbitrage there. Whereas with the general manager—by the way, the person I ended up hiring and who I'm super excited to work with is your childhood best friend, Max. Hern? Max is so great. And he would have plenty of other opportunities. There's not this weird arbitrage where you find some farmer in Argentina.
turns.zh: 区别在于，对于视频编辑，我认为存在这种套利机会：有些人——这本质上是一种“你愿意努力工作并痴迷于不断进步吗？”——他们都远超要求。但你可以在其他国家找到这样的人……而且这甚至不是工资问题。我把他们的工资提高了十倍之类的。只是要找到一个真正注重细节的人，而这里有全球套利空间。而对于总经理——顺便说一句，我最终雇到并非常兴奋与之合作的人是你儿时的好友，Max。Hern？Max 太棒了。他会有很多其他机会。没有这种奇怪的套利，比如你在阿根廷找个农民。

turns.en: But it is striking that you were looking for a while, and then it turned out Max was looking for something new. I genuinely—this is going to be like a total 12-year-old learns about the world kind of question—but I genuinely don't know how big companies hire. Because I was trying to find this person for a year, and I'm really glad about the person that ended up hiring, but it was just like, if I needed to hire a hundred people for a company, let alone a thousand people, I just do not know how to find people like this at scale.
turns.zh: 但值得注意的是，你找了一段时间，然后发现 Max 在找新机会。我真的——这完全像个 12 岁孩子了解世界的问题——但我真的不知道大公司怎么招人。因为我花了一年时间找这个人，我对最终雇到的人非常满意，但就像，如果我需要为公司招一百个人，更不用说一千个人，我就是不知道如何大规模找到这样的人。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, I think this is like the number one issue that startup CEOs have, relentlessly. The number one. And the thing I was stunned with is how it didn't seem like my platform helped that much. I got close to a thousand applications across the different rounds of publicizing it that I did, and a lot of really cool people applied, but the person that I ended up hiring was somebody who was just a reference—a mutual friend kind of thing. And a couple of other top contenders were also this way. So it's weird: the best people in the world don't want to apply, at least to things like this. And you just got to seek them out, even if you think you have a public platform or something.
turns.zh: 是啊，我是说，我认为这是创业公司 CEO 的头号问题，一直如此。头号问题。让我惊讶的是，我的平台似乎没有帮上太多忙。我通过几轮公开宣传收到了近一千份申请，很多很酷的人申请了，但我最终雇到的人只是一个推荐——一个共同朋友之类。其他几个顶级候选人也是这样的。所以很奇怪：世界上最好的人不想申请，至少对这类事情。你只能去找他们，即使你认为自己有公开平台之类的。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. I mean, the job might just be so out of distribution from anything else that people would do. That's right, yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊，是啊。我是说，这份工作可能太偏离人们通常会做的事情了。没错，对。

turns.en: So, Sora Ray asks: how do you make it on Substack as a newbie writer? Okay, I think if you were starting from scratch, there's two useful hacks. One is podcasting, because you don't need to have some super original take; you can just interview people who do and leverage their platform. And two is writing book reviews, again because you have something to react to rather than having to come up with a unique worldview of your own. There's probably other things, and it's really hard to give advice in advance; just try things. But those are just good cold starts.
turns.zh: 所以，Sora Ray 问：作为一个新手写手，如何在 Substack 上成功？好的，我认为如果你从零开始，有两个有用的技巧。一个是做播客，因为你不需要有超级原创的观点；你可以采访有观点的人，并利用他们的平台。第二个是写书评，同样因为你有东西可以回应，而不必自己想出独特的世界观。可能还有其他方法，提前给建议很难；只管尝试。但这些都是很好的冷启动方式。

turns.en: The book reviews is a good suggestion. I actually use GW's book reviews as a way to recommend books to people, by the way. This is a totally undersupplied thing. Because if anybody has book reviews, Jason Ferman is this economist who has like a thousand Goodreads reviews, and I like...
turns.zh: 书评是个好建议。顺便说一句，我实际上用 GW 的书评来向人们推荐书籍。这是一个完全供应不足的东西。因为如果有人有书评，Jason Ferman 这位经济学家有上千条 Goodreads 评论，我喜欢……

turns.en: I have visited his Goodreads, a hundred independent visits. Wow, same with the Goodreads book reviews or something, right? So book reviews are a very under-supplied thing. If you were looking to get started making some kind of content, I like that. Yeah, cool. Thank you guys so much for doing this. Yeah, this we'll turn the tables on you again pretty soon, but uh, how does it feel being in the hot seat?
turns.zh: 我访问过他的 Goodreads，有一百次独立访问。哇，Goodreads 的书评也是，对吧？所以书评是一种非常供不应求的东西。如果你想开始做某种内容，我喜欢这个。是的，酷。非常感谢你们做这个。是的，我们很快会反过来问你，但呃，坐在热椅上感觉如何？

turns.en: It's nice. Nobody ever asks me a question. Nobody ever asks. How is it? Yeah. Super excited for the book launch. The website's awesome, by the way. Appreciate it. Oh yeah, the yes. Strife.press scaling. Yeah. Um, cool, cool. Thanks guys. See you. Thanks.
turns.zh: 感觉不错。从来没人问我问题。从来没人问。怎么样？是的。超级期待新书发布。顺便说一句，网站很棒。很感激。哦，是的。Strife.press scaling。嗯，酷，酷。谢谢大家。再见。谢谢。

turns.en: Okay. I hope you enjoyed that episode. So as we talked about, my new book is out. It's released with Strife Press, called The Scaling Era, and it compiles the main insights across these last few years of doing these AI interviews. And I'm super pleased with how it turned out. It really elevates the conversations and adds the necessary context. And just seeing them all together, even reminding me of many of the most interesting segments and insights that I had myself forgotten. So I hope you check it out. Go to the link in the description below to buy it. Separately, I have a Clips Channel now on YouTube. Um, people keep complaining about the fact that I put clips and the main video on the same channel, so request granted. There is a new clips Channel, but please do subscribe to it, um, so you can get it kick-started. And while you're at it, also make sure to subscribe to the main Channel. Um, other than that, just honestly the most helpful thing you can do is share the podcast. If you enjoyed it, just send it on Twitter, put it in your group chats, share it with whoever else you think might enjoy it. Um, that's the most helpful thing. If you want to learn more about advertising on future episodes, go to dwar.cash/advertise. Okay, see you on the next one.
turns.zh: 好的。希望你喜欢这期节目。正如我们所说，我的新书已经出版了。它由 Strife Press 发行，名为《Scaling 时代》，汇集了过去几年做这些 AI 访谈的主要见解。我对结果非常满意。它确实提升了对话的层次，并增加了必要的背景。看到它们汇集在一起，甚至让我想起了许多我自己都忘记的最有趣的片段和见解。所以我希望你去看看。点击下方描述中的链接购买。另外，我现在在 YouTube 上有一个剪辑频道。嗯，人们一直抱怨我把剪辑和主视频放在同一个频道，所以请求已批准。有一个新的剪辑频道，但请订阅它，嗯，这样你可以帮助它起步。同时，也请确保订阅主频道。嗯，除此之外，老实说，你能做的最有帮助的事情就是分享这个播客。如果你喜欢它，就把它发到 Twitter 上，放到你的群聊里，分享给你认为可能喜欢的任何人。嗯，这是最有帮助的事情。如果你想了解在未来节目中做广告的更多信息，请访问 dwar.cash/advertise。好的，下期见。

consensus.en: Pre-training alone won't yield novel connections; significant RL is needed for discovery.
consensus.zh: 仅靠预训练无法产生新颖的联系；需要大量的强化学习才能实现发现。

consensus.en: Models lack long-term coherence needed for running companies; humans still essential.
consensus.zh: 模型缺乏经营公司所需的长期连贯性；人类仍然不可或缺。

consensus.en: Put yourself close to the frontier to see real problems and opportunities.
consensus.zh: 让自己靠近前沿，才能看到真正的问题和机会。

consensus.en: Content quality trumps fame; unknown guests can become most popular.
consensus.zh: 内容质量胜过名气；不知名的嘉宾也可能成为最受欢迎的。

consensus.en: Distribution is underrated; YouTube shorts drove half of podcast growth.
consensus.zh: 分发被低估了；YouTube 短视频推动了播客一半的增长。

consensus.en: Writing book reviews and podcasting are good cold starts for new writers.
consensus.zh: 写书评和做播客是新写手不错的冷启动方式。

contrarian.en: LLMs are like idiot savants: encyclopedic memory but poor generalization.
contrarian.zh: 大语言模型像白痴天才：百科全书式的记忆但泛化能力差。

contrarian.en: Slow compounding growth in media is fake; viral hits come from quality.
contrarian.zh: 媒体中的缓慢复利增长是假的；病毒式传播来自质量。

contrarian.en: Best hires come from personal referrals, not public applications.
contrarian.zh: 最好的招聘来自个人推荐，而非公开申请。

contrarian.en: Canceled 401k contributions due to fast AGI timelines; money may not matter.
contrarian.zh: 由于 AGI 时间线快而取消 401k 缴款；金钱可能不再重要。

contrarian.en: Historical figures like LBJ or Robert Moses would run AI labs better.
contrarian.zh: 像林登·约翰逊或罗伯特·摩西这样的历史人物会更善于运营 AI 实验室。

contrarian.en: Poetry is like TikTok: quick vibes, then swipe to next.
contrarian.zh: 诗歌就像 TikTok：快速感受氛围，然后滑到下一个。
